attrs.alt: CA Grant Controls decision tree showing AND/OR operator logic and all built-in controls

content.text: Grant controls are the enforcement gate in a Conditional Access policy — they define what a user must satisfy (or be denied by) before the policy allows access. Unlike session controls, which constrain behavior after access is granted, grant controls are evaluated at token issuance time.
Block: immediately denies access. No other control can override this within the same policy.
Built-in controls: a fixed set of checks Entra ID can enforce natively (MFA, device compliance, app protection, password change).
Authentication strength: a newer, finer-grained replacement for 'Require MFA' that specifies which authentication methods count.
Operator (AND / OR): when multiple controls are listed, AND requires all to be satisfied; OR requires only one.

'Block' is the most powerful grant control. When a policy matches, block overrides everything — there is no satisfying it. Use it deliberately and always exclude break-glass accounts from policies that block all users.
Satisfied by authenticator app, FIDO2, SMS, voice, etc.
Device must be managed and meet all compliance rules
Require Hybrid Azure AD joined device
On-prem AD with Azure AD Connect sync
Device must be domain-joined and synced
Enforces MAM-capable apps; being deprecated in favor of compliantApplication
Intune App Protection Policy on client
Verifies MAM policy applied to specific app; does not require device enrollment
Forces password reset before access; used for high user risk policies
Replaces 'Require MFA' for per-method granularity; supports custom strengths
Immediately denies the request; no control satisfies this — it always blocks

Authentication strength replaces the blunt 'Require MFA' control with a per-method allowlist. Three built-in strengths cover common use cases. Administrators can create custom strengths that include only specific method combinations — for example, a 'corporate-approved' strength that excludes SMS and voice OTP.
All methods satisfying MFA: Authenticator app, FIDO2, Windows Hello, SMS (+ others)
Microsoft Authenticator (passwordless), FIDO2 security key, Windows Hello for Business
FIDO2 security key, Windows Hello for Business, certificate-based auth only
Administrator selects allowed methods from the full method list

# Create a custom authentication strength allowing only Authenticator and FIDO2
$body = @{
    DisplayName = 'Corp-Approved MFA'
    Description = 'Only allows Microsoft Authenticator and FIDO2 for MFA'
    AllowedCombinations = @(
        'windowsHelloForBusiness',
        'fido2',
        'microsoftAuthenticatorPush',
        'deviceBasedPush'
    )
} | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 5

Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method POST \
    -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/policies/authenticationStrengthPolicies' \
    -Body $body -ContentType 'application/json'

# List all custom strengths and their IDs
$strengths = Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET \
    -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/policies/authenticationStrengthPolicies'
$strengths.value | Select-Object id, displayName, policyType, allowedCombinations

Phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2, WHfB, CBA) is the highest assurance level. Use it for admin portals, privileged access, and any resource handling regulated data. Migrate admin accounts from 'mfa' to 'authenticationStrength' with phishing-resistant as the path to eliminating password-based compromises.
App Protection Policy vs Approved Application

Two controls manage mobile access without requiring device enrollment: approvedApplication (legacy, targeting a Microsoft-maintained list of MAM-capable apps) and compliantApplication (current, requiring an Intune App Protection Policy to be assigned and active). Microsoft has deprecated approvedApplication for new policies.
compliantApplication supports BYOD: users can access corporate data in Outlook or Teams without enrolling their personal device.
The App Protection Policy must be assigned to the user in Intune — presence of the app alone is not sufficient.
compliantApplication does not verify device health or compliance — it only verifies the MAM policy on the specific app.
For higher security, combine compliantApplication (OR) with compliantDevice for users who have enrolled devices, allowing both paths.
Determine IF access is granted at all
Constrain WHAT a user can do once granted access
Enforced during the session in real time
Require MFA, Require compliant device, Block
Sign-in frequency, Persistent browser session, App-enforced restrictions
AND / OR operators across multiple controls
Multiple session controls stack (all apply simultaneously)
Access denied (or user must satisfy the control)
Session allowed but constrained (download blocked, re-auth required, etc.)

Identity Protection risk signals feed into CA conditions (signInRiskLevels, userRiskLevels). The recommended pattern is a three-tier policy set: block high-risk sign-ins, require MFA for medium-risk, and require password change for high user risk. Each tier is a separate CA policy to avoid conflicts and allow independent audit.

Block is the most impactful grant control — it affects every matched user immediately. Misconfigurations in block policies have caused organization-wide lockouts. Follow these safety patterns before enabling any block policy.
Emergency accounts may use legacy protocols in recovery
Block guest access to internal apps
Guest approvers need access to approve requests
New devices can't enroll if blocked before compliance check
Avoid account lockout during major incidents
Create the policy in Report-Only mode and monitor SigninLogs for 48–72 hours.
Verify the exclusion group contains all break-glass and emergency access accounts.
Enable the policy for a small pilot group (e.g., 50 users) and confirm no unintended blocks.
Gradually expand scope — by department or role — watching sign-in logs between each expansion.
Enable for all users only after pilot validation is complete.

Sign-in logs record which CA policies evaluated, whether they succeeded or failed, and which grant controls were enforced. Use KQL queries against the SigninLogs table (sent to a Log Analytics workspace) to identify policy misconfigurations and blocked users.

// KQL: find all CA grant control failures in the last 7 days
SigninLogs
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(7d)
| where ResultType != 0  // exclude success
| where ConditionalAccessStatus == 'failure'
| mv-expand ConditionalAccessPolicies
| where ConditionalAccessPolicies.result == 'failure'
| extend PolicyName = tostring(ConditionalAccessPolicies.displayName)
| extend GrantControls = tostring(ConditionalAccessPolicies.enforcedGrantControls)
| summarize FailureCount = count() by UserPrincipalName, PolicyName, GrantControls
| order by FailureCount desc

// Find users blocked by a specific policy
SigninLogs
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(24h)
| mv-expand ConditionalAccessPolicies
| where ConditionalAccessPolicies.displayName has 'Block'
| where ConditionalAccessPolicies.result == 'failure'
| distinct UserPrincipalName, IPAddress, AppDisplayName
| order by UserPrincipalName asc

Declaring CA policies in Terraform or Bicep ensures consistent grant control configuration across environments and prevents ad-hoc changes that drift from the intended security posture. The AzureAD Terraform provider exposes 'grant_controls' as a block with 'operator', 'built_in_controls', and 'authentication_strength_policy_id'.

Authentication strength IDs for built-in policies are deterministic UUIDs ending in a fixed suffix (000...0002, 000...0003, 000...0004). Hard-code these in Terraform rather than using a data source lookup — they never change.

These patterns cover the most frequent CA grant control scenarios. Deploy as separate named policies rather than combining all logic into one policy — separation makes audit, troubleshooting, and rollback far simpler.
Use role inclusion, not user inclusion
Allows either MAM-only or fully enrolled devices
Forces SSPR; both controls must be satisfied
Scope to GuestsOrExternalUsers; exclude allowed guests via group
Admin portals restricted to known, joined hardware
Use reportOnly state; monitor 48h before enabling
Auditing Grant Controls via Graph API

The Graph API returns the full grantControls object for every CA policy, including the operator, builtInControls array, and authenticationStrength reference. Use this to build an inventory of what each policy enforces and to safety-audit block policies for proper exclusions.

'compliantDevice' failures are among the most common CA support tickets. The policy evaluates the device's compliance state in Intune at sign-in time — if the device is noncompliant, in grace period, or not enrolled, the grant control fails. The fix is always on the device side, not the CA policy.

# Troubleshoot 'compliantDevice' grant failures

# Step 1: Check device compliance state in Intune
Get-MgDeviceManagementManagedDevice -Filter "userPrincipalName eq 'user@contoso.com'" |
    Select-Object DeviceName, ComplianceState, ManagementState, OperatingSystem, OsVersion

# ComplianceState values: compliant, noncompliant, unknown, notApplicable, inGracePeriod
# Only 'compliant' satisfies the compliantDevice grant control

# Step 2: Check if the device is registered/joined in Entra ID
Get-MgDevice -Filter "displayName eq 'DESKTOP-ABC123'" |
    Select-Object DisplayName, TrustType, AccountEnabled, IsCompliant, IsManaged

# TrustType: Workplace (registered), AzureAD (Entra ID joined), ServerAD (hybrid joined)

# Step 3: Force compliance re-evaluation in Intune
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method POST \
    -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/deviceManagement/managedDevices/<device-id>/syncDevice"

# After sync, wait 5-10 minutes and retry sign-in; check SigninLogs for updated result
Grace period compliance means the device has triggered a noncompliant rule but the Intune grace period has not yet expired — the grant control FAILS even during grace period.
Hybrid joined devices (TrustType = ServerAD) satisfy 'domainJoinedDevice' but NOT 'compliantDevice' unless they are also enrolled in Intune.
iOS/Android devices must have the Intune Company Portal app installed and the enrollment profile accepted before compliance state is sent.
After any Intune compliance policy change, devices can take up to 8 hours to re-evaluate — trigger a manual sync to force immediate re-check.
Grant controls define the enforcement gate: users must satisfy the specified controls or be blocked.
AND requires ALL listed controls; OR requires ANY ONE — choose based on how strict the policy must be.
Block is absolute: it cannot be satisfied and applies immediately to all matched users.
Authentication strength replaces 'Require MFA' and allows per-method granularity including phishing-resistant methods.
compliantApplication enforces Intune App Protection Policy on the client app without requiring device enrollment.
Always test block policies in Report-Only mode with break-glass exclusions before enabling them.
